did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper developed and evaluated a convolutional neural network (CNN)-based deep learning model for the early detection of skin cancer (specifically melanoma) using dermatoscopic images. The study employed transfer learning with pre-trained models (ResNet50 and VGG16) on the ISIC dataset to classify lesions as benign or malignant, achieving an accuracy of 93.85% on the validation set.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper is explicitly labeled as a 'Research Article' in the journal header. It presents original experimental work involving the development, training, and validation of a CNN-based deep learning model for skin cancer detection using the ISIC dataset. The study includes specific methodology sections (Data Collection, Methodology, Evaluation Metrics), reports original quantitative results (e.g., accuracy of 93.85% on validation set), and discusses limitations and future work, which are characteristic of primary research rather than a review.

title: Developing an Artificial Intelligence Model to Analyze Skin Images and Detect Skin Cancer in Its Early Stages
journal: Journal of Information Systems Engineering and Management
brief_summary: This study developed a convolutional neural network (CNN) model for the early detection of skin cancer, specifically melanoma, using dermatoscopic images. The researchers utilized transfer learning with pre-trained architectures, including ResNet50 and VGG16, trained on the International Skin Imaging Collaboration (ISIC) dataset containing 16,000 labeled images. The model was evaluated using accuracy, precision, recall, F1 score, and AUC-ROC metrics on training, validation, and unseen test sets. Key results showed an accuracy of 93.85% on the validation set and 92.30% on the test set, with an AUC-ROC of 0.962 on validation and 0.948 on testing. The study concludes that the model demonstrates robust discriminatory power and was successfully deployed as a web-based tool for clinical integration.

lesion_types_studied.details: Specific malignant lesion type studied; 3,000 images (18.75% of dataset) labeled as Malignant (Melanoma) in Table 1. The paper focuses on early detection of melanoma.

Mentioned in the Data Collection section as part of the dataset content. Grouped under 'Other Cancers' (1,000 images, 6.25%) in Table 1, which served as the second malignant class in the binary classification task.
12,000 images (75% of dataset) labeled as Benign in Table 1. Used as the negative class for the binary classification of benign vs. malignant.

all_task_types.target: Binary classification of skin lesions as benign or malignant
all_task_types.details: The model predicts discrete diagnostic categories (benign vs. malignant) using a CNN with transfer learning (ResNet50, VGG16) on the ISIC dataset. The task is explicitly described as classifying lesions as benign or malignant, with evaluation metrics including accuracy, precision, recall, F1 score, and AUC-ROC for distinguishing between these two classes.

datasets_used.name: International Skin Imaging Collaboration (ISIC) Archive
datasets_used.description: A large, publicly available repository containing high-resolution dermatoscopic images of skin lesions with distinct annotations provided by dermatologists. The dataset includes images from various skin types and demographics.

use.training: Used for training the CNN model (80% of the dataset, 12,800 images) with data augmentation applied to handle class imbalance.
use.validation: Used for validation (20% of the dataset, 3,200 images) to monitor performance and prevent overfitting.
use.testing: Used for testing on unseen data to evaluate generalization, though specific counts for the test split were not explicitly reported in the text.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To analyze the distribution of true positives, true negatives, false positives, and false negatives for both benign and malignant classes, specifically to identify misclassification rates (e.g., false negatives in the malignant category).
To evaluate the model's discriminatory power between benign and malignant lesions by plotting the True Positive Rate against the False Positive Rate at various threshold settings.
To quantify the overall ability of the model to distinguish between classes, providing a single scalar metric for comparison across training, validation, and test sets.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using descriptive metrics such as accuracy, precision, recall, F1 score, and AUC-ROC.

ISIC Dataset (Training Set).notes: The model was trained on 80% of the dataset (12,800 images). Data augmentation (random rotations, flips, zooms, lighting modifications) was applied. The paper notes that while there was a small decrease in validation performance compared to training, metrics remained strong, indicating no overfitting.

ISIC Dataset (Validation Set).notes: The validation set constituted 20% of the dataset (3,200 images). The AUC-ROC of 0.962 indicates strong discriminatory power. The model made relatively few false positive predictions (precision 91.70%) and effectively identified most malignant lesions (recall 92.10%).

ISIC Dataset (Test Set).notes: The test set was completely isolated during training. The drop in performance compared to validation is attributed to testing on truly unseen data. The model maintained robust generalization.

ISIC Dataset (Test Set - Confusion Matri.notes: The confusion matrix highlights 100 false negatives in the malignant category, which is a concern for delayed diagnosis. The model correctly classified 1,420 benign cases and 450 malignant cases.

architecture_used.main_model: Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) using Transfer Learning with ResNet50 and VGG16 backbones

architecture_details.layers: Early layers frozen; later layers fine-tuned. Includes convolutional layers, max-pooling layers, and fully connected layers.
architecture_details.classifier_head: Fully connected layers followed by a softmax output layer for multi-class classification (benign, malignant, other cancers)

preprocessing_techniques: Hair removal using morphological operations (erosion and dilation)
Data augmentation (random rotations up to ±30 degrees, horizontal and vertical flipping, zooming up to 20%, brightness and contrast adjustments)
Quality control via exclusion of low-quality images (significant noise, shadows, blurriness) and images lacking proper labeling

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Applied up to ±30 degrees to simulate different viewing angles.
Applied to simulate different viewing angles.
Applied up to 20% to provide variations in the lesion's size and position.
Applied to simulate different lighting conditions.
Augmented images were generated dynamically during training to ensure constant exposure to new variations.

conclusions: The developed CNN-based model using transfer learning with ResNet50 and VGG16 achieved robust performance in detecting skin cancer, with an accuracy of 93.85% on the validation set and 92.30% on the unseen test set.
The model demonstrated strong discriminatory power, evidenced by an AUC-ROC of 0.962 on the validation set and 0.948 on the test set.
The model was successfully deployed as a web-based application to facilitate real-time analysis and integration into clinical workflows via electronic health records (EHR).
A key limitation identified is the model's reliance on high-quality dermatoscopic images, which may not always be available in real-world clinical settings.
The study acknowledges that the ISIC dataset lacks sufficient representation of diverse skin types and ethnic backgrounds, limiting generalizability across different populations.
Future work will focus on enhancing the model's robustness to image quality variations and expanding its applicability to a broader variety of skin types.
important_remarks: The study relies on the ISIC dataset, which the authors acknowledge lacks sufficient representation of diverse skin types and ethnic backgrounds, limiting generalizability across different populations.
The model's performance is contingent on high-quality dermatoscopic images; its robustness to lower-quality images commonly found in real-world clinical settings was not fully addressed.
The dataset exhibits significant class imbalance (75% benign vs. 18.75% malignant), which was mitigated via data augmentation but may still introduce bias.
No formal statistical hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values) was reported; performance was evaluated using descriptive metrics only.
The paper does not report specific counts for the test set split, only providing aggregate performance metrics for the unseen test data.
The model was deployed as a web-based application with EHR integration, but external validation in real-world clinical workflows was not performed.
